title: Group isomorphism
content:
In abstract algebra, a group isomorphism is a function between two group s that sets up a one-to-one correspondence between the elements of the groups in a way that respects the given group operations. If there exists an isomorphism between two groups, then the groups are called isomorphic. From the standpoint of group theory, isomorphic groups have the same properties and need not be distinguished. Definition and notation.

Given two groups formula_1 and formula_2 a "group isomorphism" from formula_1 to formula_4 is a bijective group homomorphism from formula_5 to formula_6 Spelled out, this means that a group isomorphism is a bijective function formula_7 such that for all formula_8 and formula_9 in formula_5 it holds that formula_11 The two groups formula_1 and formula_4 are isomorphic if there exists an isomorphism from one to the other. This is written: formula_14 Often shorter and simpler notations can be used.

When the relevant group operations are unambiguous they are omitted and one writes: formula_15 Sometimes one can even simply write formula_5 = formula_6 Whether such a notation is possible without confusion or ambiguity depends on context. For example, the equals sign is not very suitable when the groups are both subgroups of the same group. See also the examples. Conversely, given a group formula_18 a set formula_19 and a bijection formula_20 we can make formula_21 a group formula_4 by defining formula_23 If formula_21 = formula_5 and formula_26 then the bijection is an automorphism ("q.v.").

Intuitively, group theorists view two isomorphic groups as follows: For every element formula_27 of a group formula_28 there exists an element formula_29 of formula_21 such that formula_29'behaves in the same way' as formula_27 (operates with other elements of the group in the same way as formula_27). For instance, if formula_27 generates formula_28 then so does formula_36 This implies in particular that formula_5 and formula_21 are in bijective correspondence. Thus, the definition of an isomorphism is quite natural. An isomorphism of groups may equivalently be defined as an invertible morphism in the category of groups, where invertible here means has a two-sided inverse. Examples.

In this section some notable examples of isomorphic groups are listed. Some groups can be proven to be isomorphic, relying on the axiom of choice, but the proof does not indicate how to construct a concrete isomorphism. Examples: Properties. The kernel of an isomorphism from formula_1 to formula_2 is always {eG} where eG is the identity of the group formula_1 If formula_1 and formula_4 are isomorphic, then formula_5 is abelian if and only if formula_21 is abelian.

If formula_68 is an isomorphism from formula_1 to formula_2 then for any formula_71 the order of formula_72 equals the order of formula_73 If formula_1 and formula_4 are isomorphic, then formula_1 is locally finite group if and only if formula_4 is locally finite. The number of distinct groups (up to isomorphism) of order formula_78 is given by sequence A000001 in OEIS. The first few numbers are 0, 1, 1, 1 and 2 meaning that 4 is the lowest order with more than one group. Cyclic groups.

All cyclic groups of a given order are isomorphic to formula_79 where formula_80 denotes addition modulo formula_81 Let formula_5 be a cyclic group and formula_78 be the order of formula_84 formula_5 is then the group generated by formula_86 We will show that formula_87 Define formula_88 so that formula_89 Clearly, formula_90 is bijective. Then formula_91 which proves that formula_92 Consequences. From the definition, it follows that any isomorphism formula_7 will map the identity element of formula_5 to the identity element of formula_19 formula_96 that it will map inverses to inverses, formula_97 and more generally, formula_98th powers to formula_78th powers, formula_100 and that the inverse map formula_101 is also a group isomorphism.

The relation "being isomorphic" satisfies all the axioms of an equivalence relation. If formula_68 is an isomorphism between two groups formula_5 and formula_19 then everything that is true about formula_5 that is only related to the group structure can be translated via formula_68 into a true ditto statement about formula_19 and vice versa. Automorphisms. An isomorphism from a group formula_1 to itself is called an automorphism of this group. Thus it is a bijection formula_109 such that formula_110 An automorphism always maps the identity to itself. The image under an automorphism of a conjugacy class is always a conjugacy class (the same or another).

The image of an element has the same order as that element. The composition of two automorphisms is again an automorphism, and with this operation the set of all automorphisms of a group formula_28 denoted by formula_112 forms itself a group, the of formula_84 For all abelian groups there is at least the automorphism that replaces the group elements by their inverses. However, in groups where all elements are equal to their inverse this is the trivial automorphism, e.g. in the Klein four-group.

For that group all permutations of the three non-identity elements are automorphisms, so the automorphism group is isomorphic to formula_114 and formula_115 In formula_116 for a prime number formula_117 one non-identity element can be replaced by any other, with corresponding changes in the other elements. The automorphism group is isomorphic to formula_118 For example, for formula_119 multiplying all elements of formula_120 by 3, modulo 7, is an automorphism of order 6 in the automorphism group, because formula_121 while lower powers do not give 1. Thus this automorphism generates formula_122 There is one more automorphism with this property: multiplying all elements of formula_120 by 5, modulo 7.

Therefore, these two correspond to the elements 1 and 5 of formula_124 in that order or conversely. The automorphism group of formula_125 is isomorphic to formula_126 because only each of the two elements 1 and 5 generate formula_124 so apart from the identity we can only interchange these. The automorphism group of formula_128 has order 168, as can be found as follows. All 7 non-identity elements play the same role, so we can choose which plays the role of formula_129 Any of the remaining 6 can be chosen to play the role of (0,1,0).

This determines which corresponds to formula_130 For formula_131 we can choose from 4, which determines the rest. Thus we have formula_132 automorphisms. They correspond to those of the Fano plane, of which the 7 points correspond to the 7 non-identity elements. The lines connecting three points correspond to the group operation: formula_133 on one line means formula_134 formula_135 and formula_136 See also general linear group over finite fields. For abelian groups all automorphisms except the trivial one are called outer automorphism s. Non-abelian groups have a non-trivial inner automorphism group, and possibly also outer automorphisms.
